speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really unwell since yesterday—fever, chest tightness, and my blood sugar has been all over the place.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your labs from this morning—your hemoglobin is 159 g/L, which is within normal range for you.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history, ECG, and prior angiogram, your primary diagnosis remains Coronary Artery Disease.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve had stable angina for years, but this feels different—more acute, and I’m sweating a lot.

speaker: doctor
text: I see your temperature is elevated at 39.4 °C—this suggests an acute infectious or inflammatory process that could be stressing your cardiac system.

speaker: patient
text: I did have a sore throat and cough starting two days ago—I thought it was just a cold.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important. Given your CAD and current febrile state, we need to rule out myocarditis or sepsis-related cardiac stress.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Insulin glargine 10U nightly—let’s hold that today because of your elevated glucose and acute illness.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose this morning was 10.0 mmol/L—higher than your usual fasting target, likely due to infection-induced insulin resistance.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 123 bpm—tachycardic, which aligns with fever and possible volume depletion or cardiac strain.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been drinking water, but I haven’t eaten much—my appetite’s gone completely.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll check CRP, blood cultures, and a troponin. Also, let’s get a chest X-ray to assess for pneumonia, given your fever and respiratory symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: Should I still take my aspirin and atorvastatin?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—continue those. But hold the glargine until your fever breaks and glucose stabilizes. We’ll switch you to rapid-acting insulin sliding scale while you’re acutely ill.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. And what about my chest pain? It’s worse when I lie flat.

speaker: doctor
text: That positional component raises concern for pericarditis—we’ll add an echocardiogram to the workup.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked again this morning before coming in, my blood sugar was 12.4 mmol/L—so higher than that reading from earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: That confirms significant hyperglycemia under stress—I’ll adjust your insulin regimen accordingly once we have more data.

speaker: patient
text: And the heart rate—last night it was around 98, but now it’s up to 123. That number really worries me.

speaker: doctor
text: It does—and it’s why we’re acting quickly. I’m admitting you for observation, IV antibiotics pending culture results, and cardiac monitoring.
